Design Thinking Research
Sections 1–15 · 2024
Research & Design Thinking Initiative — Hyderabad, India

MindBridge

Understanding Dementia in Older Indian Women

A human-centered research study exploring the lived realities of elderly women in Hyderabad navigating cognitive decline — and those who care for them.

Research Project Empathy Driven India Focused Hyderabad Context
Explore Research ↓
Scroll
About the Project

A study rooted in
care and context

MindBridge is an empathy-led UX research project focused on understanding the daily lived experiences of older Indian women affected by dementia — and the caregivers and family members who support them across Hyderabad.

This study explores cognitive decline not just as a medical condition, but as a deeply human experience shaped by culture, gender, language, and access to healthcare.

"Dementia is not just a memory disease — it is a cultural, social, and deeply personal journey, especially for Indian families navigating care within limited systems."

  • Understand barriers to early diagnosis in urban Indian households
  • Document caregiver emotional burdens in Hyderabad families
  • Explore cultural stigma and its effect on help-seeking behaviour
  • Identify gaps in accessible mental health infrastructure
16.9M Seniors projected to be affected by dementia in India
60–70% Of people with dementia live in lower-income countries, including India
<5% Of dementia cases in India currently receive formal diagnosis or support
Invisible Most cases remain hidden within family caregiving systems
Hyderabad Primary research context — where eldercare traditions meet rapidly changing urban lifestyles, and dementia awareness remains critically underacknowledged

Our shared vision for this research

We believe that meaningful design research begins not with assumptions, but with genuine curiosity. This project brought together six different perspectives — cultural, emotional, analytical, and visual — to understand a condition that touches millions of Indian families, yet remains largely invisible in public conversation.

6

Researchers working across
field, synthesis, and design

Understanding the Condition

What is Dementia?

A group of symptoms affecting memory, thinking, and behaviour — dementia is not a normal part of ageing.

Dementia is caused by diseases that damage brain cells — most commonly Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, or Lewy body dementia. As the condition progresses, it affects a person's ability to live independently and maintain their sense of self.

In the Indian context, dementia is often dismissed as "old age forgetfulness" — a perception that delays diagnosis and places enormous silent burden on families who have no framework for what they are experiencing.

In Hyderabad and across urban India, awareness is growing — but slowly. Families are often left navigating this alone, without access to geriatric specialists or community support.

Early detection matters. Recognising symptoms early allows families to plan, access support, and preserve quality of life for both the person living with dementia and those caring for them.

With early detection, care, and compassion, we can protect their memories and their dignity — not just as patients, but as mothers, grandmothers, and lifelong anchors of their families.

— From our field research, Hyderabad
🧠
Memory Loss

Forgetting recent events, names, and familiar places — often the earliest and most distressing sign

🔁
Confusion

Difficulty understanding time, place, and sequence — becoming disoriented in familiar surroundings

💬
Communication

Struggling to find words, follow conversations, or express needs clearly in daily interactions

🌊
Mood Changes

Increased anxiety, depression, and emotional volatility — often misread as personality changes

🏡
Daily Activities

Difficulty with routine tasks like cooking, managing money, or navigating familiar routes

~55M

People globally are living with dementia today — making it one of the most urgent aging health challenges worldwide

Source: World Health Organization

60–70%

Of those affected live in low- and middle-income countries where formal diagnosis and specialist care remain limited

Source: World Health Organization

Invisible

The vast majority of dementia cases in India remain undiagnosed — hidden within family caregiving systems with no external support

From our research context, Hyderabad

Research Focus

Why elderly
Indian women?

Older Indian women face a compounded set of vulnerabilities when it comes to dementia — rooted in biology, culture, social structure, and decades of caregiving. Our research is specifically centered on this group because their experience is disproportionately invisible.

⚖️

The Caregiving Burden

In Indian households, women are traditionally expected to be the primary caregivers. When dementia arrives, the same women who spent decades caring for others often find themselves without a support system when they need care themselves.

🏥

Healthcare Accessibility

Geriatric specialists and dementia-trained clinicians remain rare in India. Older women, particularly those without independent income or decision-making power, are especially unlikely to receive timely assessment or referral.

🤫

Social Stigma & Silence

In many Hyderabad families, dementia is spoken about in hushed tones — if at all. Stigma attached to cognitive conditions means families delay seeking help and avoid community conversations, especially for elderly women whose dignity is tied to cognitive competence.

🧵

Cultural Expectations

Indian cultural frameworks celebrate the elder woman's role as keeper of tradition and memory. When dementia disrupts this role — when a grandmother forgets prayers she has recited for sixty years — families experience grief, confusion, and shame simultaneously.

💔

Emotional Isolation

Women living with dementia in Hyderabad often describe a growing sense of invisibility. Caregivers too experience profound emotional isolation, lacking access to counseling, peer communities, or respite care.

🔬

Biological Risk Factors

Women are statistically more vulnerable to dementia due to longer life expectancy, hormonal changes, and higher rates of depression. In the Indian context, these risks are compounded by limited access to preventive healthcare and lower rates of formal education.

📊
Women

Are disproportionately affected by dementia globally and in India — reflecting longer lifespan and compounded risk factors specific to women in the Indian context

🏙️
Hyderabad

As one of India's fastest-growing cities, Hyderabad presents a critical intersection of traditional family caregiving norms and rapidly changing urban lifestyles — creating new pressures on eldercare systems

Field Research

Darus Suffa — A Visit That Changed Us

Darus Suffa Aram Ghar · Hyderabad
⤢

Visited Darus Suffa Old Age Home in Hyderabad as part of our dementia research study. The visit brought us face to face with elderly women navigating daily life under the weight of cognitive decline.

During our visit, we observed emotional isolation, memory-related struggles, dependency on caregivers, and the deep importance of empathy in elderly care. The interaction helped us understand how dementia affects not only memory, but also emotional wellbeing, relationships, independence, and dignity.

Field Notes — Mahindra University, Hyderabad

"Empathy begins when we stop observing people as subjects and start listening to them as humans."

💬 Emotional Isolation

Many residents showed visible longing for family connection and meaningful conversation

🧠 Memory Struggles

Repeated questions, disorientation in familiar spaces, and difficulty with daily routines

🤝 Caregiver Dependency

High reliance on staff for basic activities while independence gradually diminishes

🌿 Need for Dignity

Residents responded warmly to respectful interaction — dignity matters as much as care

Voices from the Field

Stories that
shaped our research

Through conversations with caregivers, families, and elderly residents, we gathered perspectives that no textbook could provide. These voices form the emotional foundation of our research.

Interview · 01
Caregiver Perspective
"She needs constant support now."

A caregiver reflects on the growing complexity of daily care as dementia progresses — and the emotional toll it takes on those who give it.

▶ Listen Interview
Interview · 02
Daily Struggles
"I sometimes forget where I keep things."

An elderly resident shares the disorienting experience of navigating daily life when familiar routines begin to blur and slip away.

▶ Listen Interview
Interview · 03
Emotional Reflection
"It becomes emotionally difficult for families."

A family member speaks candidly about the emotional exhaustion of watching a loved one change — and the silence that often surrounds it.

▶ Listen Interview
Interview · 04
Elderly Experience
"We just want them to feel safe."

A simple but profound wish — safety, dignity, and warmth — expressed by those who have watched the people they love navigate this condition.

▶ Listen Interview
Expert Insight

Neurologist Interview — Dr. Aseema

To better understand dementia medically and neurologically, we conducted an interview with a neurologist discussing cognitive decline, Alzheimer's disease, emotional wellbeing, social isolation, synapses, and the importance of brain health in elderly women.

Interviewed by
Bushra Kausar

"Dementia is much more than a memory problem."

"Social interaction itself can be protective for the brain."

"Families often normalize symptoms as aging."

"Brain health and heart health are deeply connected."

Bushra
Ma'am basically mera topic dementia aur cognitive decline ke baare mein hai, especially focused on elderly women. Honestly mujhe pehle itna knowledge nai tha, but jab research karna start kiya toh I realized it's much more serious than what people think.
Dr. Aseema
Yes, absolutely. Bohot log dementia ko sirf "memory problem" samajhte hain, lekin actually it is a disease that gradually affects the brain over time. Normally our brain has billions of neurons — brain cells — that continuously communicate. They help with memory, language, emotions, decision-making… basically daily functioning. As people age, these cells become more vulnerable to damage.
Dr. Aseema
In diseases like Alzheimer's, abnormal proteins start building up in the brain. Over time these proteins interfere with communication between neurons, and eventually some brain cells stop functioning properly and die. Synapses — the connection points between neurons — are among the earliest affected. Before brain cells even completely die, their communication starts weakening.
Dr. Aseema
Normal aging aur dementia mein difference hai. Occasionally forgetting where you put your keys is common. But repeatedly forgetting familiar people, repeating the same question many times, ya confusion in daily tasks — that becomes concerning. Especially elderly women ke case mein, symptoms are often normalized. Families say "umar hogya."
Dr. Aseema
Poor vascular health — conditions like hypertension, diabetes, high cholesterol — can damage blood vessels and affect cognition. That is why heart health aur brain health are deeply connected. Sleep also plays a very important role. During sleep the brain clears metabolic waste products and restores itself. Stress and depression can also contribute.
Dr. Aseema
Women statistically have higher rates of Alzheimer's disease compared to men — partly because women generally live longer, and researchers are studying hormonal influences after menopause. And social interaction itself is protective for the brain. Conversation, routine interaction, emotional connection — all of these stimulate the brain. Loneliness and isolation can worsen cognitive decline and depression.
Dr. Aseema
I remember one patient — around seventy years old. She had managed her household independently for decades. Initially she started forgetting ingredients while cooking. Eventually one day she completely forgot a pressure cooker on the stove. That was when the family became concerned — and she was diagnosed with early Alzheimer's disease. Early diagnosis is still very important. Even without a complete cure, treatment and support can improve quality of life significantly.
Empathy & Experience

Empathy Journey Map

The journey map helped us visualize the emotional, behavioral, and practical experiences faced by elderly women living with dementia and the caregivers supporting them.

By mapping different emotional stages, routines, frustrations, and dependencies, we were able to better understand how dementia affects daily life beyond medical symptoms.

Confusion
Forgetfulness
Dependency
Emotional Isolation
Need for Support

Understanding emotional journeys allows more empathetic and human-centered solutions to emerge.

Click to enlarge
Systems Thinking

Root Cause Analysis

Click to enlarge

Our root cause analysis identified the deeper systemic and social factors contributing to dementia-related challenges faced by elderly Indian women.

The analysis revealed connections between healthcare accessibility, emotional isolation, social stigma, financial limitations, caregiving pressure, and lack of awareness within communities.

Rather than viewing dementia only as a medical condition, this exercise helped us understand the broader emotional, cultural, and societal dimensions surrounding cognitive decline.

Social Stigma Healthcare Gaps Gender Inequality Emotional Isolation Financial Burden Lack of Awareness

Meaningful solutions require addressing both medical and social dimensions of dementia care.

Design Thinking

6-3-5 Ideation Process

Using the 6-3-5 brainstorming method, our team explored multiple ideas focused on dementia awareness, emotional wellbeing, caregiver support, safety, memory assistance, and independent living for elderly women in Hyderabad. The activity encouraged collaborative thinking, rapid idea generation, and exploration of practical community-centered solutions.

📄
6-3-5 Brainstorming Document
108 ideas · 6 participants · 6 rounds

This document contains our complete 6-3-5 brainstorming process, collaborative ideation rounds, dementia-care concepts, awareness strategies, caregiver support systems, and safety-focused solutions explored during the design thinking process.

Open Full Ideation PDF ↗
How Might We…
HMW

Support elderly women with dementia in maintaining independence in daily routines?

HMW

Reduce caregiving burden while ensuring quality care for both families and patients?

HMW

Create safer home and community environments for dementia patients?

HMW

Improve awareness and reduce stigma around dementia in Indian communities?

Memory Wall
📅
Daily Routine Tracker
About the 6-3-5 Method

6 participants each write 3 ideas every 5 minutes, then pass their sheet to the next person. Ideas are built upon, expanded, and combined across rounds — generating a rich pool of diverse, community-centered solutions.

6
Participants
3
Ideas/Round
5
Min/Round
108+
Total Ideas
Design Thinking Process

How we approached the problem

Design thinking helped us approach dementia not only as a medical issue, but also as a deeply human-centered challenge.

01
🫂

Empathize

Understanding emotions, struggles, and caregiver experiences through interviews and field visits.

Field Research
→
02
🎯

Define

Identifying key problems related to awareness, emotional wellbeing, healthcare access, and caregiving burden.

Problem Framing
→
03

Ideate

Exploring multiple solution ideas through collaborative brainstorming and 6-3-5 ideation.

Brainstorming
→
04
🔧

Prototype

Developing simple support concepts focused on safety, reminders, emotional care, and assistance.

Solution Design
→
05
✅

Test

Evaluating whether the proposed ideas could realistically support elderly women and caregivers.

Validation

"Design thinking helped us approach dementia not only as a medical issue, but also as a deeply human-centered challenge."

Prototype Solution

From ideas to concepts

Short-Term Solution

Memory Mitra

⊕ Zoom
Memory Mitra — Awareness Poster

Memory Mitra is a festival-based dementia awareness and early screening initiative designed for elderly Indian women.

The concept uses culturally familiar memory activities and community interaction to identify possible cognitive decline in a comfortable and non-clinical environment.

Key Highlights
✦ Festival booth setup at community gatherings
✦ Cultural memory activities in familiar language
✦ ASHA worker-led gentle observation
✦ Family guidance and awareness support
Long-Term Vision

MindWell Communities

MindWell Communities focuses on long-term emotional wellbeing, dementia awareness, family support, stress reduction, and healthier community engagement for women.

The initiative encourages emotional support systems, mental wellbeing awareness, and healthier lifestyles through community-centered activities.

Community Focus
✦ Women's awareness sessions
✦ Family and community education
✦ Stress management and wellness
✦ Long-term cognitive health support
⊕ Zoom
MindWell Communities — Long-Term Vision
⏱ Short-Term — Memory Mitra
Early awareness
Community screening
Memory engagement
Family guidance
🌱 Long-Term — MindWell Communities
Emotional wellbeing

Key Insights & Learnings

What we discovered

01

"Dementia affects emotional wellbeing and relationships as deeply as memory."

02

"Many symptoms are normalized as aging, delaying awareness and support."

03

"Social interaction plays a major role in emotional and cognitive wellbeing."

04

"Caregivers experience emotional stress alongside patients."

05

"Empathy-driven observation provides deeper understanding than statistics alone."

Team Reflection

Our field visits, interviews, and research interactions helped us move beyond theoretical understanding and recognize the emotional realities surrounding dementia care in elderly Indian women.

Final Reflection

A journey in empathy

MindBridge began as a research project, but gradually became an exploration of empathy, dignity, aging, memory, and human connection.

Through conversations, observations, and field experiences, we realized that dementia care extends beyond medicine — it is also about emotional support, understanding, patience, and community.

Creating awareness and encouraging compassionate care can help elderly women live with greater dignity, safety, and emotional wellbeing.

"

Sometimes the most meaningful support begins simply by listening.
